h1Title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Abnormal uterine bleeding is any deviation from normal menstruation in frequency, regularity, duration, or volume, and is the presenting complaint in approximately 20-30% of all gynecology consultations. The FIGO PALM-COEIN classification system, published in 2011 and updated in 2018, identifies nine categories of structural and non-structural causes that affect women from menarche through menopause. Worldwide, approximately 30% of reproductive-age women experience abnormal uterine bleeding at some point, with heavy menstrual bleeding being the single most common gynecological reason for anemia requiring iron therapy. Bleeding that is new-onset after menopause carries a 10-15% risk of endometrial carcinoma and requires investigation the same day it is reported. Effective treatments — from hormonal suppression to endometrial ablation and surgery — now offer alternatives to hysterectomy in the majority of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 30% of reproductive-age women experience abnormal uterine bleeding at some point; heavy menstrual bleeding affects 20-30% of women (NICE NG88 2018)
keyStats.demographics: Reproductive-age women 15-49 are most affected; postmenopausal bleeding requires urgent investigation regardless of volume; adolescents presenting with heavy first menses commonly have von Willebrand disease (up to 13% of cases)
keyStats.avgAge: Heavy menstrual bleeding peak age 40-50 (perimenopausal); postmenopausal bleeding most concerning after age 55
keyStats.globalCases: Heavy menstrual bleeding is a leading cause of iron deficiency anaemia globally, contributing to anaemia in an estimated 470 million women worldwide

definition: Abnormal uterine bleeding (ICD-10: N93.9 — Abnormal uterine and vaginal bleeding, unspecified) encompasses any uterine bleeding that falls outside the normal parameters: cycle length 24-38 days, flow duration 4-8 days, and volume less than 80 mL per cycle. The FIGO PALM-COEIN system classifies causes into structural (Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia) and non-structural (Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified) categories. Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) is defined as blood loss above 80 mL per cycle or bleeding that significantly impacts quality of life; intermenstrual bleeding (IMB) is bleeding between periods; and postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) is any uterine bleeding occurring 12 or more months after the final menstrual period. Each pattern has a different differential diagnosis, workup, and urgency. The condition is managed by gynecologists, with oncology input for malignant causes, hematology input for coagulopathy, and endocrinology input for thyroid and ovulatory disorders. Untreated chronic heavy bleeding leads to iron deficiency anaemia in over 40% of affected women and substantially impairs quality of life, productivity, and sexual function.

typesClassification.description: Cyclically regular, heavy flow — more than 80 mL per cycle or significantly impacting quality of life. The most common subtype; caused by uterine fibroids, adenomyosis, polyps, coagulopathies, or ovulatory dysfunction. Formerly termed menorrhagia.

Bleeding occurring between regular menstrual periods. Causes include cervical polyps, cervical ectropion or carcinoma, endometrial polyps, chlamydial cervicitis, and ovulatory spotting at mid-cycle. Any IMB requires speculum examination and cervical screening.

Any uterine bleeding 12 or more months after the last menstrual period. Requires urgent investigation; endometrial carcinoma is found in 10-15% of cases. Other causes include vaginal atrophy, endometrial polyps, and endometrial hyperplasia.
Cycles varying by more than 7-9 days with unpredictable timing. Usually reflects ovulatory dysfunction from PCOS, hyperprolactinemia, thyroid disease, or perimenopause.

typesClassification.type: Abnormal Bleeding Related to Hormonal Contraception or HRT
typesClassification.description: Unscheduled bleeding within 3-6 months of starting oral contraceptives, hormonal IUDs, implants, or hormone replacement therapy. Usually represents normal breakthrough bleeding or suboptimal endometrial suppression but requires evaluation to exclude pathology.

primarySymptoms: Heavier than usual menstrual flow requiring frequent pad or tampon changes (more than one every 2 hours for several consecutive hours), passage of blood clots larger than a 50-cent coin, or bleeding so heavy it interferes with daily activities.
Cycle length falling below 21 days (polymenorrhea) or extending beyond 35 days (oligomenorrhea) in a woman whose previous cycles were regular.
Periods lasting longer than 7-8 days, with persistent spotting continuing after the main flow should have ended.
Bleeding occurring between periods — mid-cycle spotting, post-coital bleeding (bleeding after sex), or completely unpredictable bleeding unrelated to any cycle pattern.
Any vaginal bleeding occurring more than 12 months after the final menstrual period in a woman who has entered menopause — this symptom requires same-week gynecological evaluation.
Fatigue, breathlessness on exertion, and pallor from iron deficiency anaemia caused by chronic blood loss exceeding the body's iron stores.
Pelvic pressure or fullness, urinary frequency, or difficulty emptying the bowel in women with heavy bleeding from large uterine fibroids compressing adjacent structures.

Cyclic pain that worsens with menstruation (dysmenorrhea) in women with abnormal bleeding from adenomyosis or endometriosis — both conditions frequently cause a combination of heavy and painful periods.
Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding from cuts, or a family history of bleeding disorders in adolescents or young women with very heavy first menses, raising suspicion for underlying coagulopathy.
Unintended weight gain, cold intolerance, or hair loss alongside heavy periods, suggesting hypothyroidism as the underlying cause of ovulatory dysfunction and abnormal bleeding.
earlyWarningSigns: Passage of blood clots larger than 2.5 cm during any menstrual period — indicates blood loss exceeding the uterine capacity to lyse clots, a recognized marker of heavy menstrual bleeding
Regular need to use two forms of menstrual protection simultaneously (pad and tampon) to prevent leakage through clothing
Breakthrough bleeding on a hormonal contraceptive beyond 6 months of use — may indicate fibroids, polyps, or suboptimal contraceptive compliance that warrants evaluation
Any vaginal bleeding in a pregnant woman, particularly in the first trimester — requires same-day assessment to exclude ectopic pregnancy or miscarriage
emergencySigns: Heavy bleeding soaking more than one large pad per hour for two consecutive hours with associated dizziness, pallor, or syncope — haemodynamic compromise requiring emergency attendance
Any postmenopausal bleeding in a woman who has not been on HRT — 10-15% risk of endometrial carcinoma requiring urgent (same-week) gynecological evaluation with pelvic ultrasound and biopsy
Profuse bleeding in pregnancy with abdominal pain — may represent placental abruption, placenta praevia, or ruptured ectopic; obstetric emergency requiring immediate hospital attendance

Heavy vaginal bleeding in an adolescent with signs of haemodynamic instability (heart rate above 120, BP below 90/60) — may indicate a severe bleeding disorder (platelet disorder, von Willebrand disease type 3) requiring haematological evaluation alongside gynaecology

causes.description: The most common structural cause of heavy menstrual bleeding, found in 70-80% of women by age 50. Submucosal fibroids protruding into the uterine cavity increase surface area of the endometrium, impair uterine contractility during menstruation, and cause persistent heavy flow. Intramural fibroids of more than 4 cm similarly contribute to heavy bleeding.

Endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium cause uterine enlargement, increased vascularity, and impaired myometrial contraction — the mechanism of hemostasis after menstruation. Results in heavy, painful periods; affects 20-35% of women of reproductive age, with higher rates in women over 40. Diagnosed by MRI or transvaginal ultrasound.

Benign outgrowths of the endometrium on a fibrovascular stalk; present in 2-12% of women with AUB. Cause intermenstrual bleeding and heavy periods by disrupting normal endometrial architecture. The majority are benign but malignant transformation occurs in 0.5-3.2%, particularly in postmenopausal women on tamoxifen.

Anovulatory cycles lack the progesterone production of the luteal phase; unopposed estrogen drives endometrial proliferation without organized shedding, causing irregular, often heavy, unpredictable bleeding. PCOS is the most common cause; hypothyroidism and hyperprolactinemia also impair ovulation. Prolonged anovulation raises endometrial cancer risk.

Endometrial carcinoma is found in 10-15% of postmenopausal women presenting with bleeding and in 5% of perimenopausal women with persistent irregular bleeding. Atypical endometrial hyperplasia carries a 30% risk of coexisting endometrial carcinoma and requires hysterectomy or progestogen therapy. Obesity, diabetes, and unopposed estrogen are the main risk factors.

Congenital bleeding disorders cause heavy menstrual bleeding in up to 13% of adolescents presenting with their first heavy periods. Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited coagulation disorder overall; ITP (immune thrombocytopenic purpura) and platelet function disorders are less common causes. Acquired coagulopathy from anticoagulant therapy (warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants) is an increasingly common iatrogenic cause in adults.

riskFactors.description: Adipose tissue converts androgens to estrogens (aromatization), creating a state of relative estrogen excess and progesterone deficiency that drives endometrial proliferation and irregular bleeding. Obesity also doubles the risk of endometrial carcinoma independent of menstrual pattern.

Chronic anovulation in PCOS produces unopposed estrogen stimulation of the endometrium, causing irregular heavy bleeding and long-term endometrial hyperplasia risk. Affects 5-10% of reproductive-age women and accounts for a significant proportion of anovulatory AUB.

Tamoxifen acts as a partial estrogen agonist in the uterus, stimulating endometrial proliferation. Risk of endometrial polyps (20-40% after 5 years), endometrial hyperplasia, and endometrial carcinoma (relative risk 2-3×) is increased; any vaginal bleeding in a woman on tamoxifen requires urgent pelvic ultrasound and endometrial biopsy.

Anticoagulation increases menstrual blood loss by 20-50% on average; many women on anticoagulants have menorrhagia as a direct consequence. Tranexamic acid and hormonal suppression can reduce loss without reversing anticoagulation. Requires close monitoring and gynecological involvement.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of uterine fibroids or endometrial cancer
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of women with fibroids have a 2-3 fold elevated risk. Hereditary endometrial cancer syndromes (Lynch syndrome) cause endometrial carcinoma risk approaching 60% lifetime; any woman under 50 with endometrial cancer should be referred for genetic counseling.

Women who have not been pregnant have a higher lifetime estrogen exposure relative to progesterone and higher rates of endometrial hyperplasia, fibroids, and endometrial carcinoma compared with parous women.

Hormonal fluctuations during the menopausal transition cause anovulatory cycles in 40-80% of women, producing irregular, often heavy, unpredictable bleeding that can be difficult to distinguish clinically from pathological causes without endometrial sampling.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents (aged 12-20) with heavy first menses — up to 13% have an underlying coagulopathy (von Willebrand disease); hormonal irregularity in the first 2-3 years after menarche is also common.
Reproductive-age women aged 30-50 — fibroids and adenomyosis account for the majority of structural heavy menstrual bleeding in this group.

Perimenopausal women aged 45-55 — anovulatory cycles, endometrial polyps, and early endometrial hyperplasia are the main concerns; endometrial sampling is required for persistent or irregular bleeding.
Postmenopausal women aged 55 and above — any bleeding requires urgent evaluation; endometrial carcinoma risk rises with age and is the primary concern.
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation of abnormal uterine bleeding begins with a targeted history: onset, pattern (heavy, intermenstrual, postcoital, postmenopausal), cycle regularity, associated symptoms (pain, discharge, systemic), medications (anticoagulants, hormonal agents, tamoxifen), and reproductive and cancer history. Pregnancy must be excluded with a serum or urine beta-hCG in all reproductive-age women. The physical examination includes speculum visualization of the cervix (to detect cervical polyps, ectropion, or cervical malignancy), bimanual pelvic examination (to assess uterine size, shape, and tenderness), and a cervical smear if due. Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) is the first-line imaging study for all AUB — it assesses endometrial thickness (the key predictor of malignancy), myometrial and cervical morphology, ovarian morphology, and detects fibroids and polyps with high sensitivity. Endometrial biopsy (Pipelle aspirate in an office setting) is the primary investigation for postmenopausal bleeding and for perimenopausal women with a thickened endometrium (above 4 mm after menopause), persistent irregular bleeding, or risk factors for endometrial carcinoma. NICE NG12 (2015) recommends endometrial biopsy for all women with postmenopausal bleeding and any woman with AUB over 45 with unexplained persistent bleeding. Saline infusion sonohysterography or office hysteroscopy improves detection of intracavitary pathology (polyps, submucosal fibroids) beyond TVUS alone. Full blood count detects anaemia from chronic loss. Coagulation screen (aPTT, PT, fibrinogen, von Willebrand factor antigen, and ristocetin cofactor) is sent in adolescents with heavy first periods and in women with lifelong menorrhagia without structural cause.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging for AUB; assesses endometrial thickness, uterine morphology, fibroids, adenomyosis, and ovarian pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Probe gently inserted into the vagina for 10-15 minutes. No radiation. Endometrial thickness above 4 mm in postmenopausal women triggers biopsy. Most women find it tolerable; abdominal ultrasound used as an alternative if TVUS is declined or not feasible.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Obtains endometrial tissue for histology to detect carcinoma, atypical hyperplasia, or simple hyperplasia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible tube is passed through the cervix; mild cramping for 30-60 seconds while a small sample is aspirated. Office procedure with no anaesthesia needed in most cases. Results in 5-10 working days. Routine for postmenopausal bleeding and perimenopausal women with risk factors.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the uterine cavity to detect polyps, submucosal fibroids, uterine adhesions, and focal endometrial lesions, with targeted biopsy when abnormalities are seen
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A narrow telescope is passed through the cervix. Outpatient with local anaesthetic in most cases; general anaesthesia for operative cases. Provides definitive structural diagnosis and simultaneous polypectomy. Takes 10-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and iron studies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies anaemia from chronic blood loss; serum ferritin and transferrin saturation assess iron stores before clinical anaemia develops
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 2-4 hours. Haemoglobin below 12 g/dL with low MCV and ferritin confirms iron deficiency from menstrual loss. Guides urgency of treatment and need for iron supplementation.

diagnosticTests.test: Coagulation screen and von Willebrand panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes inherited bleeding disorders in adolescents with heavy first periods and in women with lifelong menorrhagia without identified structural cause
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; von Willebrand panel requires processing at a specialist coagulation laboratory; results in 5-7 days. Includes aPTT, PT, fibrinogen, VWF antigen, VWF ristocetin cofactor, and factor VIII. Performed after excluding NSAID use that can affect results.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distends the uterine cavity with saline during ultrasound to better visualise polyps and submucosal fibroids than standard TVUS alone
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small catheter placed through the cervix; saline slowly infused; ultrasound performed simultaneously. Mild cramping. No radiation. Takes 15-20 minutes. Significantly increases polyp detection sensitivity from 60-70% to over 90% compared with TVUS alone.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of abnormal uterine bleeding targets the underlying cause while managing symptoms — heavy bleeding, anaemia, pain — and minimizing the impact on quality of life and fertility. NICE NG88 (2018) and the FIGO AUB Guidelines provide the evidence base for a stepwise, cause-directed approach. For heavy menstrual bleeding without structural cause, first-line medical treatment is the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS, Mirena), which reduces menstrual blood loss by 71-95% at 3-6 months and has the strongest evidence base of any available intervention. Oral hormonal options include combined oral contraceptive pills, cyclic progestogen (norethisterone 5 mg three times daily from day 5-26), and tranexamic acid, an antifibrinolytic agent that reduces blood loss by 26-54% without hormonal effect, making it suitable for women who cannot use estrogen. NSAIDs (mefenamic acid 500 mg three times daily during menstruation) reduce loss by 20-40% and simultaneously treat dysmenorrhea. For structural causes, hysteroscopic polypectomy removes endometrial polyps with high success and provides definitive histology; uterine artery embolization (UAE) or myomectomy addresses fibroid-related HMB while preserving the uterus; and endometrial ablation treats treatment-resistant HMB without structural cause as a day-case procedure, achieving satisfactory bleeding control in 80-90% of women. GnRH agonists (leuprolide 3.75 mg monthly) are used short-term to shrink fibroids pre-operatively and correct anaemia before surgery. Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia is managed with a 6-month course of high-dose progestogen (medroxyprogesterone acetate 10-20 mg daily or LNG-IUS); atypical hyperplasia requires hysterectomy given 30% risk of concurrent endometrial carcinoma. Postmenopausal bleeding from vaginal atrophy responds to topical oestrogen cream. Any woman with confirmed endometrial malignancy requires urgent referral to a gynecological oncologist. Iron deficiency anaemia from chronic blood loss is corrected with oral ferrous sulfate or intravenous iron infusion before elective surgical procedures.

medicalTreatments.name: Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS, Mirena)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding without structural cause per NICE NG88. Releases 20 µg levonorgestrel per day, suppresses endometrial proliferation, and reduces menstrual blood loss by 71-95%. Also provides contraception and reduces dysmenorrhea. Inserted in outpatient or operating-theatre setting; effective for 5-8 years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 71-95% reduction in menstrual blood loss at 3-6 months; satisfaction rates equivalent to or exceeding surgical options in RCTs (Lethaby et al. Cochrane 2015)

medicalTreatments.name: Tranexamic acid (1 g three times daily during menstruation)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-hormonal antifibrinolytic that inhibits plasminogen activation, reducing fibrinolysis and menstrual blood loss. Taken only during heavy bleeding days (up to 5 days per cycle). No effect on cycle regularity or fertility. First-line option when hormonal therapy is declined.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces menstrual blood loss by 26-54% compared with placebo; superior to NSAIDs and comparable to combined OCP in head-to-head trials (Lukes et al. Obstet Gynecol 2010)

medicalTreatments.name: Combined oral contraceptive pill (30 µg ethinyl estradiol with norethisterone or levonorgestrel)
medicalTreatments.description: Suppresses endometrial growth and regulates ovulation, reducing menstrual blood loss by 43% on average and providing predictable cyclical bleeding. Second-line option for HMB when LNG-IUS is not preferred. Requires contraindication screening (migraine with aura, VTE risk, cardiovascular risk factors).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Average 43% reduction in menstrual blood loss; cycle regulation in over 90% of users; also treats dysmenorrhea and premenstrual symptoms

medicalTreatments.name: Progestogen therapy (norethisterone 5 mg twice or three times daily days 5-26, or high-dose MPA for hyperplasia)
medicalTreatments.description: Cyclic oral progestogen induces regular withdrawal bleeds and suppresses endometrial proliferation. Used for ovulatory dysfunction-related AUB, endometrial hyperplasia management (MPA 10-20 mg daily), and as a short-term measure awaiting definitive surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Regression of simple endometrial hyperplasia in 80-90% with 6-12 months of LNG-IUS or oral progestogen; less effective than LNG-IUS for HMB suppression (Lethaby et al. Cochrane 2019)

medicalTreatments.name: Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 200 mg twice daily or IV ferric carboxymaltose)
medicalTreatments.description: Restores depleted iron stores in women with anaemia from chronic blood loss. Oral iron is first-line; IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, 1-2 infusions) is preferred before surgery or when oral iron is poorly tolerated or absorbed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haemoglobin rises by 1-2 g/dL per month with oral iron; IV iron restores haemoglobin and iron stores within 4-8 weeks, enabling earlier surgery without transfusion

medicalTreatments.name: GnRH agonists (leuprolide 3.75 mg monthly, or nafarelin nasal) for fibroid-related HMB
medicalTreatments.description: Induce temporary medical menopause, shrinking uterine fibroids by 35-65% over 3-6 months, reducing blood loss to minimal or absent. Used pre-operatively to shrink fibroids, correct anaemia, and facilitate surgery. Add-back HRT prevents bone loss in courses exceeding 6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Amenorrhoea in 80-95% of users; fibroid volume reduction of 35-65%; haemoglobin recovery allows conversion of previously impossible myomectomy to feasible outpatient procedure

surgicalOptions.name: Hysteroscopic polypectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of endometrial or cervical polyps under direct hysteroscopic vision, usually in outpatient hysteroscopy or day-surgery. Permanent histology is obtained from all removed polyps to exclude malignancy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of intermenstrual bleeding and reduction of heavy flow in 75-90% of women with symptomatic polyps; polypectomy preferred over watchful waiting given 0.5-3.2% malignant transformation risk

surgicalOptions.name: Myomectomy (hysteroscopic or laparoscopic/open)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of fibroids while preserving the uterus. Hysteroscopic myomectomy treats submucosal fibroids (FIGO type 0-2) with high success; laparoscopic or open myomectomy for larger intramural or subserosal fibroids. Suitable for women who wish to retain fertility.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Heavy bleeding resolved in 70-80% at 1 year; fibroid recurrence rate 15-30% at 5 years, higher in women with multiple fibroids

surgicalOptions.name: Endometrial ablation
surgicalOptions.description: Destroys the endometrial lining using resection, thermal balloon, microwave, or rollerball techniques. Suitable for women with no structural cause and completed childbearing. Provides long-term heavy bleeding control without hysterectomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Amenorrhoea in 30-50% and satisfactory bleeding control in 80-90% of women at 5 years; avoidance of hysterectomy in 70-80% of cases at 10 years (Cooper et al. BMJ 2011)

surgicalOptions.name: Uterine artery embolization (UAE)
surgicalOptions.description: Interventional radiological procedure in which microspheres are injected into both uterine arteries via a femoral artery catheter, cutting off blood supply to fibroids. Day-case or overnight stay. Preserves the uterus; not recommended if fertility is desired.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant symptom improvement in 85-90% at 1 year; 20-30% require further intervention (repeat UAE or hysterectomy) within 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Hysterectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for HMB when all other treatments have failed or are declined. Total hysterectomy (removal of uterus and cervix) via laparoscopic, vaginal, or open approach. Guarantees cure of uterine-origin bleeding.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 100% resolution of abnormal uterine bleeding; patient satisfaction rates of 85-95% at 2 years; associated with longer recovery (2-6 weeks) and definitive loss of fertility

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture for heavy menstrual bleeding
alternativeTreatments.description: Small RCTs show modest reduction in blood loss with traditional acupuncture; evidence quality is low. May be an adjunct for women who decline pharmacological or surgical treatment but should not be used as first-line or sole therapy given the importance of excluding malignancy and treating anaemia.

whySeeSpecialist: Any woman with postmenopausal bleeding should be seen by a gynecologist within 2 weeks to exclude endometrial carcinoma. Women with heavy menstrual bleeding that is not responding to initial medical management within 3-6 months, women with suspected structural pathology on ultrasound, and women who are significantly anaemic or have impaired quality of life should be referred for gynecological assessment including pelvic ultrasound, endometrial assessment, and consideration of hysteroscopy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a gynecologist with experience in hysteroscopy, endometrial biopsy, and minimally invasive surgery (myomectomy, ablation). Women with suspected endometrial carcinoma need rapid access to a gynecological oncologist. Adolescents with heavy first periods and suspected coagulopathy benefit from a combined gynecology and haematology assessment. Women wishing to preserve fertility should see a specialist in reproductive surgery.
hospitalCriteria: Haemodynamically unstable heavy vaginal bleeding with dizziness, syncope, or hypotension
Newly diagnosed postmenopausal bleeding requiring urgent pelvic ultrasound and endometrial biopsy within 2 weeks
Haemoglobin below 8 g/dL with symptomatic anaemia from chronic menstrual blood loss before elective surgical procedure
Suspected uterine or cervical malignancy requiring rapid-access gynecological oncology assessment
keyFacilities: Outpatient hysteroscopy units in gynecology departments
Radiology units with uterine artery embolization capability
Gynecological oncology centres for malignancy investigation and management
Haematology services for coagulopathy workup in adolescents
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy weight — obesity amplifies estrogen exposure and risk of anovulatory bleeding and endometrial hyperplasia; even 5-10% body weight reduction improves menstrual regularity in overweight women.
Use hormonal contraception (LNG-IUS, combined OCP) under guidance when structural or ovulatory AUB is present — both protect the endometrium and reduce heavy bleeding.
Report any vaginal bleeding after menopause immediately to a physician — early investigation dramatically improves outcomes for endometrial carcinoma.
Attend regular cervical screening — cervical carcinoma, which causes postcoital and intermenstrual bleeding, is largely preventable through HPV vaccination and screening.
Manage insulin resistance and blood glucose in women with PCOS or type 2 diabetes — glycaemic control improves ovulatory frequency and reduces endometrial hyperplasia risk.
Monitor women on long-term tamoxifen with annual pelvic ultrasound and immediate biopsy of any endometrial thickening or vaginal bleeding.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a menstrual diary tracking volume (pad count, clot size), cycle length, and associated symptoms to provide objective data to the care team.
Take prescribed iron supplementation consistently — ferrous sulfate with vitamin C improves absorption; take on an empty stomach if tolerated.
Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs (except under medical direction with appropriate bleeding monitoring), which can worsen blood loss in already heavy periods.
Reduce excessive alcohol intake — alcohol impairs liver metabolism of estrogens, contributing to estrogen excess and irregular bleeding.
Practice pelvic floor exercises — relevant for women whose AUB follows pelvic surgery or childbirth and who have coexisting prolapse or incontinence.
Attend all follow-up appointments for endometrial surveillance, particularly if prior endometrial hyperplasia has been diagnosed.

recommended: Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, liver, legumes, fortified cereals, dark leafy greens) consumed with vitamin C to enhance absorption and counteract menstrual iron losses
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, guava, peppers, broccoli) to enhance non-haem iron absorption from plant sources
Folate-containing foods (legumes, spinach, asparagus) to support red blood cell production in women with anaemia
Omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) — some evidence for modest reduction in prostaglandin-mediated dysmenorrhea and potential modest reduction in menstrual blood loss
avoid: Excessive alcohol (more than 14 units per week) — impairs hepatic estrogen metabolism, worsening estrogen excess and irregular bleeding
Very high dietary phytoestrogens in large supplement doses — possible endometrial stimulation in susceptible women, though evidence at food-equivalent doses is reassuring
Caffeine in excess — limited evidence of direct effect on bleeding, but can worsen anxiety and premenstrual symptoms
High-sodium processed foods that worsen fluid retention and bloating around menstruation

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week, such as brisk walking, swimming, or cycling) reduces insulin resistance, improves anovulatory bleeding in PCOS, and supports weight management. However, very high-intensity training can suppress the HPO axis and cause oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea in lean athletes. Women with anaemia from heavy menstrual bleeding should avoid strenuous exercise until haemoglobin is above 10 g/dL.
dailyManagement: Track menstrual blood loss with a validated pictorial chart (PBAC score) to quantify response to treatment at follow-up visits.
Take iron supplementation consistently — 200 mg ferrous sulfate twice daily is the standard replacement dose; full iron store repletion typically takes 3-6 months after haemoglobin normalizes.
Use the LNG-IUS correctly — insertion cramping resolves within a few days; report persistent heavy bleeding beyond 6 months of use, as IUS position should be checked.
Report any post-coital bleeding immediately — cervical causes (carcinoma, polyp, ectropion) require speculum examination and colposcopy if indicated.
Record any bleeding occurring after 12 months of no periods — even a single episode of postmenopausal bleeding requires same-week medical review.
Attend scheduled hysteroscopy or endometrial surveillance follow-ups for diagnosed hyperplasia without missing appointments.
prognosis: Prognosis for abnormal uterine bleeding is generally excellent when structural and malignant causes are excluded. Heavy menstrual bleeding from fibroids, adenomyosis, or ovulatory dysfunction responds to medical management (LNG-IUS, tranexamic acid, hormonal therapy) in 70-90% of women and to surgical management when needed. Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia regresses with progestogen therapy in 80-90% of women within 6-12 months of treatment; regression should be confirmed by repeat biopsy. Atypical hyperplasia managed with hysterectomy has an excellent cure rate of over 95% given that the malignant potential is removed. Endometrial carcinoma confined to the uterus has a 5-year survival rate of 95%; stage III disease falls to approximately 45% — emphasizing the critical importance of acting on postmenopausal bleeding without delay. Iron deficiency anaemia from chronic heavy bleeding reverses within 3-6 months of bleeding control plus iron replacement in the overwhelming majority of women.
recoveryTimeline: LNG-IUS for HMB: significant reduction in blood loss by 3 months; maximum benefit by 6 months. Endometrial ablation: lighter periods from the first cycle post-procedure; full assessment of response at 3-6 months. Hysteroscopic polypectomy: menstrual normalisation expected within 1-3 cycles. Myomectomy: bleeding improvement within 1-2 cycles; recovery from laparoscopic procedure 1-2 weeks; open procedure 4-6 weeks. UAE: pain resolves over 2 weeks; menstrual improvement over 3-6 months.
complications: Iron deficiency anaemia with fatigue, cognitive impairment, and reduced work capacity — affects over 40% of women with untreated heavy menstrual bleeding; requires concurrent iron replacement alongside bleeding control.

Endometrial hyperplasia progressing to carcinoma — simple hyperplasia carries 1% malignancy risk; complex atypical hyperplasia carries up to 30% risk of concurrent endometrial carcinoma when left untreated.
Fibroids enlarging and causing urinary frequency, constipation, and pelvic pressure, or requiring emergency hysterectomy for intractable bleeding if medical management fails.
Subfertility — submucosal fibroids, polyps, and endometrial adhesions from chronic AUB reduce implantation rates and increase miscarriage risk in women trying to conceive.
Postoperative complications from hysterectomy or myomectomy — infection, haemorrhage, bladder or ureteral injury — occur in 2-5% of procedures at specialist centres.

Psychological impact — heavy menstrual bleeding significantly impairs quality of life, sexual function, work attendance, and self-esteem in a majority of affected women; these dimensions should be explicitly assessed and addressed.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG) Patient Information
supportResources.description: RCOG patient leaflets covering heavy menstrual bleeding, endometrial ablation, fibroids, and hysterectomy with balanced evidence-based summaries.

supportResources.name: NICE Patient Decision Aid: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding
supportResources.description: NICE NG88 guideline with patient-facing shared decision-making tools for comparing medical and surgical treatment options.

supportResources.name: National Society for Menstrual Disorders (US)
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy information and resources for women with heavy, painful, or irregular periods including information on endometriosis and adenomyosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amenorrhea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amenorrhea is the complete absence of menstrual periods, whereas abnormal uterine bleeding involves excess, irregular, or misplaced bleeding. Both can result from the same underlying conditions (PCOS, thyroid disease, POI) at different points in the disease process. The diagnostic workup overlaps for hormonal causes but diverges for structural causes — a thickened endometrium on ultrasound raises cancer concern in AUB, while in amenorrhea the concern is estrogen deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Endometriosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Endometriosis causes heavy, painful periods and sometimes intermenstrual spotting, overlapping significantly with AUB from adenomyosis or fibroids. However, endometriosis is defined by ectopic endometrial implants outside the uterus, confirmed by laparoscopy. It causes severe dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and deep pelvic pain disproportionate to bleeding volume, distinguishing it clinically from fibroids or simple ovulatory AUB.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS causes oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea in most cases, with episodes of heavy anovulatory bleeding when the endometrium builds up. The hormonal pattern (elevated LH:FSH, hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance) and polycystic ovary morphology distinguish it from structural AUB causes. PCOS-related AUB is managed primarily through ovulation induction or hormonal endometrial protection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Uterine Fibroids
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibroids are the most common structural cause of HMB and are part of the PALM-COEIN classification of AUB. They are diagnosed by ultrasound showing distinct myometrial masses. Treatment options for fibroid-related AUB (UAE, myomectomy, LNG-IUS) differ from those for non-structural AUB. Fibroids with submucosal extension into the uterine cavity cause the most severe bleeding.

relatedConditions.name: Uterine Fibroids
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common structural cause of heavy menstrual bleeding; fibroid treatment is the most frequently performed gynecological surgery for AUB.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Endometriosis and adenomyosis frequently coexist and both contribute to heavy painful periods; shared pathways make joint management common.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Anovulatory PCOS is a leading non-structural cause of AUB; endometrial protection is the priority alongside ovulation induction when fertility is desired.

relatedConditions.name: Amenorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposing menstrual disorder sharing many underlying hormonal causes; understanding both is essential for a complete HPO axis evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Insulin resistance drives anovulation in PCOS-related AUB; obesity and diabetes are the dominant risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma in women with AUB.

faqs.question: What is abnormal uterine bleeding?
faqs.answer: Abnormal uterine bleeding is any uterine bleeding that falls outside normal parameters: cycles shorter than 24 or longer than 38 days, flow lasting more than 8 days, blood loss above 80 mL per cycle, or bleeding between periods or after menopause. Pregnancy is excluded first; then structural causes (fibroids, polyps, cancer) and hormonal causes are investigated.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for heavy periods?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if periods regularly require more than one pad or tampon per hour for two consecutive hours, if you pass clots larger than a 50-cent coin, if periods last longer than 7-8 days, if you feel dizzy or short of breath during menstruation, or if heavy periods are interfering with daily life. Any postmenopausal bleeding requires same-week medical assessment.

faqs.question: What causes heavy periods?
faqs.answer: Uterine fibroids and adenomyosis are the most common structural causes of heavy periods. Non-structural causes include ovulatory dysfunction from PCOS, thyroid disease or high prolactin, coagulation disorders (von Willebrand disease), and anticoagulant medication. A thorough evaluation including pelvic ultrasound and blood tests identifies the cause in the majority of women.

faqs.question: Is postmenopausal bleeding serious?
faqs.answer: Postmenopausal bleeding requires urgent evaluation — it carries a 10-15% risk of endometrial carcinoma. Any bleeding occurring 12 or more months after the last period should be assessed within 2 weeks with pelvic ultrasound and, in most cases, endometrial biopsy. Most causes are benign (atrophic vaginitis, polyps), but cancer must be excluded first.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for heavy periods?
faqs.answer: The levonorgestrel-releasing IUS (Mirena) is the most effective medical treatment, reducing blood loss by 71-95% and recommended as first-line by NICE. Non-hormonal alternatives include tranexamic acid (reduces loss by 26-54%) and NSAIDs. For fibroid-related bleeding, surgical options include myomectomy or uterine artery embolization. Endometrial ablation and hysterectomy are effective for women who have completed their families.

faqs.question: Can fibroids cause abnormal bleeding?
faqs.answer: Yes. Uterine fibroids — particularly submucosal fibroids that protrude into the uterine cavity — are the most common structural cause of heavy menstrual bleeding. They increase the surface area of the endometrium and impair uterine contractile hemostasis. Fibroids are detected by pelvic ultrasound and treated by myomectomy, uterine artery embolization, GnRH agonists, or LNG-IUS.

faqs.question: Can abnormal uterine bleeding be a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Postmenopausal bleeding carries a 10-15% risk of endometrial carcinoma. In perimenopausal women with persistent irregular bleeding and risk factors (obesity, diabetes, tamoxifen use), the risk of carcinoma or atypical hyperplasia is 3-5%. Endometrial biopsy is required to exclude malignancy. In reproductive-age women with regular heavy periods, cancer is uncommon but should still be considered in those with persistent symptoms.

faqs.question: Does abnormal uterine bleeding affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Submucosal fibroids, endometrial polyps, and intrauterine adhesions reduce implantation rates and increase miscarriage risk. Anovulatory AUB from PCOS prevents natural conception. Treating the underlying structural or hormonal cause significantly improves fertility outcomes. Women wanting to conceive should discuss their options with a reproductive specialist.

faqs.question: What is PALM-COEIN?
faqs.answer: PALM-COEIN is the FIGO classification of causes of abnormal uterine bleeding: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia (structural causes) and Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial disorder, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified (non-structural causes). The system guides systematic workup and treatment selection by identifying the specific category of cause.

faqs.question: Can thyroid disease cause irregular periods?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypothyroidism elevates TRH, which stimulates prolactin, suppressing GnRH and ovulation. It commonly causes heavy or irregular periods; less often it causes amenorrhea. Hyperthyroidism typically causes lighter or absent periods. Correcting thyroid function with levothyroxine or antithyroid drugs normalizes menstrual patterns in most women within 3-6 months.

faqs.question: What blood tests are used to diagnose abnormal uterine bleeding?
faqs.answer: Standard blood tests include full blood count (detects anaemia), thyroid function (TSH and free T4), serum beta-hCG (exclude pregnancy), and in adolescents with heavy first menses, a coagulation screen and von Willebrand factor panel. Ovarian hormones (FSH, LH, estradiol, prolactin) are added when ovulatory dysfunction is suspected.

faqs.question: Is intermenstrual bleeding (spotting between periods) normal?
faqs.answer: Mid-cycle spotting at ovulation is common and benign. However, irregular intermenstrual bleeding lasting more than 2-3 cycles warrants a speculum examination and pelvic ultrasound to exclude cervical polyps, cervical ectropion, endometrial polyps, chlamydial cervicitis, and cervical carcinoma. Post-coital bleeding (spotting after sex) always warrants cervical examination.

faqs.question: Can heavy periods be a sign of a bleeding disorder?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 13% of adolescents presenting with heavy first menstrual periods have an underlying bleeding disorder, most commonly von Willebrand disease. Signs that suggest a coagulopathy include heavy bleeding since menarche, frequent bruising, prolonged bleeding from cuts, and a family history of bleeding disorders. A haematology referral and VWF panel are indicated.

faqs.question: Does obesity cause abnormal uterine bleeding?
faqs.answer: Obesity amplifies estrogen production through aromatization of androgens in adipose tissue, creating relative estrogen excess and progesterone deficiency. This drives endometrial proliferation, anovulatory irregular bleeding, and endometrial hyperplasia. Even 5-10% weight loss improves menstrual regularity in overweight women with PCOS-related AUB.

faqs.question: What is endometrial ablation and who is it for?
faqs.answer: Endometrial ablation destroys the uterine lining using heat, microwave, or electrical energy to permanently reduce menstrual bleeding. It is suitable for women with heavy menstrual bleeding who have completed childbearing and have no structural cause requiring surgery. Amenorrhoea occurs in 30-50% of women; significant bleeding reduction occurs in 80-90%. It is a day-case procedure under general or local anaesthesia.

faqs.question: How is abnormal uterine bleeding diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation, assessing endometrial thickness, fibroids, polyps, and ovarian morphology. Endometrial biopsy is performed for postmenopausal bleeding or when the endometrium is thickened. Hysteroscopy provides direct visualization and biopsy of intracavitary lesions. Blood tests detect anaemia, coagulation disorders, and thyroid or hormonal abnormalities.

simpleName: Abnormal uterine bleeding (heavy or irregular periods)
metaTitle: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding: Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
metaDescription: Abnormal uterine bleeding causes heavy, irregular, or postmenopausal bleeding. PALM-COEIN causes, diagnosis, LNG-IUS and surgical treatment options reviewed.
